label: Instruction-Tuning
definition: A form of supervised fine-tuning on instructions paired with desired responses, which teaches a base model to follow natural-language commands rather than merely continue text. Its headline result is generalization: trained on a broad mix of instruction types, models follow new kinds of instruction they were never shown, the zero-shot leap that made modern assistants possible.

body.text: Instruction-tuning teaches a model to do what it is told. A freshly pretrained base model is excellent at continuing text but has no concept of an instruction: ask it a question and it may simply generate more questions, because that is a plausible continuation. Instruction-tuning fixes this by fine-tuning the model on many examples of an instruction paired with a high-quality response, across a wide variety of tasks, after which the model interprets a prompt as a command to be carried out rather than a passage to be extended. Because it is supervised fine-tuning on demonstration pairs, it is often called SFT.

Instruction-tuning matters because it is the bridge from raw capability to usable assistant, and it is usually the first step of post-training. It unlocks two things at once: it makes the model controllable through natural language, and it confers a striking degree of generalization, the keeper. Trained to follow a broad mix of instruction types, models follow new kinds of instruction they were never explicitly shown, and this zero-shot generalization across tasks is one of the most important empirical findings behind modern assistants, a direct expression of transfer learning.

Mechanically, instruction-tuning is ordinary supervised fine-tuning. The training data is a collection of (instruction, response) pairs, sometimes including a context or input, and the model is trained with the usual next-token cross-entropy loss to reproduce the response given the instruction. The art is largely in the data: diversity of tasks, quality and consistency of the responses, and breadth of phrasing matter more than raw quantity, and datasets are built from human-written demonstrations, from templated conversions of existing labeled datasets into instruction form, and increasingly from responses generated by a stronger model.

Instruction-tuning teaches a model to follow instructions, but it does not by itself teach which of many valid responses humans most prefer, nor strong refusal of harmful requests. Those finer behaviors come from the preference-alignment stage, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or direct preference optimization (DPO), which almost always builds on an instruction-tuned starting point, and Constitutional AI similarly assumes an instruction-following model it can prompt to critique and revise its own outputs.

In the standard post-training recipe, instruction-tuning comes first and preference alignment comes second: pretrain, then instruction-tune, then align. It depends on pretraining for its knowledge and on fine-tuning as its mechanism, and it is the prerequisite that makes RLHF and DPO possible. Grasping instruction-tuning is key to understanding why a base model and a chat model behave so differently despite sharing most of their weights.

image.alt: A worked instruction-tuning task. A boxed Definition states the task in natural language, a Positive Examples box lists input-output pairs with explanations as demonstrations, and an Evaluation Instances box shows new inputs for which the model produces outputs, illustrating a single task expressed entirely as a written instruction plus examples.
image.caption: Instruction tuning trains a model on many tasks each expressed as a natural-language instruction: a definition plus a few demonstration examples, paired with the desired responses. Trained across a broad mix of such tasks, the model learns to read a prompt as a command to carry out and generalizes to unseen instruction types it was never explicitly shown, the behavior that turns a base model into a usable assistant.
image.credit: Yizhong Wang, Swaroop Mishra, Yeganeh Kordi, et al.
